title: Share of deaths from suicide
subtitle: Estimated share of deaths from suicide. Suicides are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. caption:
Share of deaths from suicide
Estimated share of deaths from suicide. Suicides are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.

Across seven nations from 1980 to 2023, the estimated share of deaths attributed to suicide varies significantly, with South Korea displaying a distinct trajectory compared to Western counterparts. The vertical axis measures the percentage of total deaths, ranging from 0% to over 6%, while the horizontal axis tracks time in five-year increments. South Korea begins the period in the 2% to 3% band, remaining relatively flat until the early 1990s, before rising steadily to cross the 4% threshold around 1998. The line continues an upward trend to reach a peak above 6% near 2010, followed by a consistent decline to approximately 4% by 2023. In contrast, the United States shows a gradual increase from roughly 1.5% in 1980 to just under 2% in 2023. Japan starts higher than the US, fluctuating between 2.5% and 3.5% through the 1980s and 1990s, then dropping below 2% by the end of the timeline. Germany follows a similar downward path from the 3% band to roughly 1.5%. The United Kingdom and Greece remain in the lower bands throughout, with the UK hovering near 1% and Greece staying below 0.5% for most of the period. Peru shows a slight rise from near 0% to roughly 0.7% before leveling off. The data shows South Korea experiencing the only major surge and subsequent fall in suicide share among the displayed countries, while other nations exhibit more stable or slowly changing patterns within lower percentage ranges.
